coredata.dc:title: Smart pH-responsive magnetic graphene quantum dots nanocarriers for anticancer drug delivery of curcumin
coredata.dc:creator: Seyyedi Zadeh, Elham
Ghanbari, Narges
Salehi, Zeinab
Derakhti, Sorour
Amoabediny, Ghassem
Akbari, Maryam
Asadi Tokmedash, Mohammad
coredata.dc:description: In this study, we designed a targeted multifunctional anticancer drug carrier based on magnetic to improve the efficiency of curcumin (Cur) as a non-toxic and hydrophobic anticancer medicine. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were synthesized from graphene oxide (GO), and then magnetic nanocomposite was prepared with chemical co-precipitation of Fe2+ and Fe3+. As a cancer-targeting agent, folic acid (FA) was conjugated and named GQDs-Fe3O4-FA nanocomposite. FTIR, VSM, XRD, and FE-SEM analyses were used to characterize the as-synthesized nanocomposite. Fourier transformed infrared (FTIR) results revealed that GQDs-Fe3O4 was synthesized, and FA was attached to it. Furthermore, according to the VSM analysis, it can be seen that GQDs-Fe3O4 has superparamagnetic behavior (Ms = 60.6 emu/g), which is shown that an applied magnetic field can steer it to the cancerous tumor site without damaging the healthy tissues. The cumulative release behavior of the drug (Cur) was monitored in phosphate buffered saline (PBS, pH 5.5 and 7.4) for 150 h. It was observed that 33% and 15% of total curcumin were released at pH 5.5 and 7.5, respectively. In vitro cytotoxicity of GQDs-Fe3O4 and Cur/GQDs-Fe3O4-FA were investigated against MCF-7 and MG-63 cells by the MTT-Assay test. According to an in vitro cytotoxicity assay, these cell lines are more vulnerable to Cur/GQDs-Fe3O4-FA than Cur/GQDs-Fe3O4, which revealed the selective role of folic acid as a targeting agent in nanocarriers. The results also showed nontoxicity of the bare nanocarrier on normal cells. Therefore, GQDs-Fe3O4-FA can be a potential and compelling candidate for therapeutic applications, including drug delivery systems.

head.ce:title: Smart pH-responsive magnetic graphene quantum dots nanocarriers for anticancer drug delivery of curcumin

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Biochemical and Pharmaceutical Engineering, School of Chemical Engineering, College of Engineering, University of Tehran, Iran

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Biochemical and Pharmaceutical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemical Engineering, College of Engineering, University of Tehran, Tehran, Iran

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, we designed a targeted multifunctional anticancer drug carrier based on magnetic to improve the efficiency of curcumin (Cur) as a non-toxic and hydrophobic anticancer medicine. Graphene quantum dots (GQDs) were synthesized from graphene oxide (GO), and then magnetic nanocomposite was prepared with chemical co-precipitation of Fe2+ and Fe3+. As a cancer-targeting agent, folic acid (FA) was conjugated and named GQDs-Fe3O4-FA nanocomposite. FTIR, VSM, XRD, and FE-SEM analyses were used to characterize the as-synthesized nanocomposite. Fourier transformed infrared (FTIR) results revealed that GQDs-Fe3O4 was synthesized, and FA was attached to it. Furthermore, according to the VSM analysis, it can be seen that GQDs-Fe3O4 has superparamagnetic behavior (Ms = 60.6 emu/g), which is shown that an applied magnetic field can steer it to the cancerous tumor site without damaging the healthy tissues. The cumulative release behavior of the drug (Cur) was monitored in phosphate buffered saline (PBS, pH 5.5 and 7.4) for 150 h. It was observed that 33% and 15% of total curcumin were released at pH 5.5 and 7.5, respectively. In vitro cytotoxicity of GQDs-Fe3O4 and Cur/GQDs-Fe3O4-FA were investigated against MCF-7 and MG-63 cells by the MTT-Assay test. According to an in vitro cytotoxicity assay, these cell lines are more vulnerable to Cur/GQDs-Fe3O4-FA than Cur/GQDs-Fe3O4, which revealed the selective role of folic acid as a targeting agent in nanocarriers. The results also showed nontoxicity of the bare nanocarrier on normal cells. Therefore, GQDs-Fe3O4-FA can be a potential and compelling candidate for therapeutic applications, including drug delivery systems.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: As a life-threatening disease, cancer is regarded as one of the significant reasons for death around the world [1]. One of the most common methods of treating cancer is chemotherapy. The drugs applied in chemotherapy, such as doxorubicin, paclitaxel, 5-fluorouracil (5-FU), and vincristine, are highly invasive and damage healthy cells besides cancer cells. Therefore, researchers tend to find less toxic drugs to defeat this problem. Curcumin has recently drawn a lot of attention as an anticancer, anti-inflammatory, and antioxidant [2–6] Curcumin is a polyphenol derived from the Curcuma longa plant [7]. Despite its unique biological properties and therapeutic potential, it has very poor water solubility, is chemically unstable under physiological conditions, and has inferior efficacy [8]. Additionally, curcumin is hardly ever absorbed by the body when taken orally. Intravenous injection allows for the raising of the maximal serum curcumin level. However, the use of curcumin by intravenous injection is limited by its low water solubility (less than 7 mg/l of water). Moreover, it is unstable in physiological pH conditions (90% degradation to tetrahydro curcumin in 30 min) [5]. Curcumin nanomedicines are a recommended method for increasing the active ingredient's bioavailability. Typical procedures for clinical drug applications include infusion directly into the bloodstream, oral delivery, and in situ drug release at the disease site. Oral administration is not a viable tactic for curcumin, though. There are two primary subcategories of nanocurcumin: those that can be administered intravenously and those that can be implanted in the body. In some postoperative therapies or cases of tissue injury, the in situ release of medicine may be preferable to intravenous injection. However, intravenous injection is the preferred method for treating many disorders, including cancer and neuroinflammation [9]. Curcumin's poor water solubility, degradation, and low bioavailability are significant barriers to its medical application, despite its considerable potential for disease treatment [10]. Curcumin's low stability and solubility in aqueous environments have been addressed through heat treatment [11] and the identification of natural water-soluble curcumin [10,12]. Developing delivery vehicles capable of transporting curcumin to in vivo target sites has been another method for improving its therapeutic applicability. These include curcumin-casein micelles [13] such as carriers including poly (lactic-co-glycolic acid) (PLGA) nanoparticles [14], micellar polymer aggregates [15], polyvinyl alcohol hydrogels [16], and pluronic block copolymer micelles [17]. A promising approach to defeat these obstacles is the application of nanoparticle-based carriers [18]. Several studies have indicated that administering curcumin via nanoparticles increases the drug's solubility, stability, and half-life in blood circulation [6–8,19–21].

Up to now, different types of nanoparticles, including liposome [22], niosome [23,24], lipid-based nanoparticles [25], and polymeric nanoparticles [26], have been studied to improve the therapeutic efficacy of curcumin [27]. However, using these nanoparticles entails some limitations. For instance, synthesizing liposomes and niosomes is expensive and difficult to scale. Also, the depolymeric nanoparticles' degradation rate is slow, possibly leading to toxicity [28]. Magnetic nanoparticles (MNPs) have attained considerable attention in numerous research fields, including magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) [29,30], tumor hyperthermia [31,32], and targeted drug delivery [33,34]. Among magnetic nanoparticles, iron oxide nanoparticles like Fe3O4 (magnetite) and γ-Fe2O3 (magmehit) have been more interested because of their distinct characteristics. They have exceptional biocompatibility and magnetic properties as well as low toxicity. Besides, their synthesis method is simple; however, some properties of these nanoparticles, such as the tendency to aggregation and precipitation, can cause serious problems [30,35–38]. The hybridization of Fe3O4 with different particles, such as graphene quantum dots (GQDs), leads to obtaining novel nanomaterials with the advantages of both components, including a decrease in aggregation and precipitation [39]. As a new generation of graphene-based nanomaterials, GQDs have one or few-layered sheets with a width of less than 100 nm [40]. They benefit from high solubility, excellent biocompatibility, high drug loading capacity, photoluminescence, and low [41–43]. Moreover, because of their abundance of carboxyl and hydroxy groups, they can be functionalized with other compounds and molecules [44]. Despite all these features and benefits, the difficult and costly separation of GQDs has posed a challenging issue for researchers in recent years. Combining GQDs with magnetic nanomaterials provides nanoparticles with comfortable separation under a magnetic field. Hence, GQDs-Fe3O4 hybrid nanoparticles have improved characteristics that make them suitable for a variety of applications, including drug delivery, fluorescence imaging, magnetic resonance imaging (MRI) [45–49], and catalysis [40,50,51]. Su et al. fabricated a novel graphene quantum dot (GQDs) conjugated with Fe3O4@SiO2 nanoprobe applicable in fluorescence/MRI dual imaging and delivery of DOX to cancer cells [46]. According to the results of another study, GQD-Concanavalin A@Fe3O4 can serve as a versatile nanocomposite for drug delivery and cancer cell detection [45]. Pooresmaeil et al. developed a new anticancer drug delivery system using Fe3O4/GQDs-gelatin. According to their results, this hybrid has a high performance as a non-toxic and pH-sensitive carrier for cancer treatment [39].

Over the last few decades, the development of targeted drug delivery systems for the treatment of cancer has been a major focus of research. These smart systems deliver a certain amount of drugs to a targeted site in the human body [52]. This mechanism increases the therapeutic effect of the drug while decreasing its adverse effects [53]. In other words, active targeted drug delivery is a method in which the targeting agent conjugates to the nanocarrier and delivers drugs through some receptors on/in a cell [11]. Various targeting agents such as folic acid [14,15], tryptophan [54], aptamers [55,56], and monoclonal antibodies [57] have been used for this purpose. On a wide variety of human cancer cells, it has been found that folate receptors are over-expressed [15]. Therefore, folic acid (FA) conjugation h the nanocarrier has been considered a high-performance strategy in targeted cancer treatments. Several studies have confirmed that the incorporation of FA in the formulation of various drug delivery systems like niosomal nanocarrier [58,59], nanoliposome [60], superparamagnetic nanoparticles [61], and magnetic mesoporous silica nanoparticles (MMSNs) [62] enhanced the cytotoxicity of the anticancer drugs loaded in these nanoparticles on cancerous cells.

You et al. observed that the folic acid-conjugated PLGA-PEG nano-niosome exhibited higher anticancer efficiency than the free drug [58]. Similarly, Moghimipour et al. demonstrated that FA bonding to a liposomal formulation improved the cellular uptake and decreased the IC50 of 5-FU-loaded nanoparticles [60]. Akbarzadeh et al. fabricated a folic acid-functionalized niosomal nanoparticle with significant inhibitory effects on MCF-7 and MDA-MB-231 breast cancer cell lines. Additionally, it was demonstrated that the conjugation of FA to superparamagnetic iron oxide nanoparticles (SPIONs) provided a smart drug carrier that could selectively target MCF-7 cells [61]. It is worth emphasizing that the nanocarrier used in our work – GQDs-Fe3O4 - has advantages compared to noisome nanoparticles, such as the simpler and lower manufacturing method. In another study by Avedian et al. it was shown that magnetic mesoporous silica nanoparticles (MMSNs)- Fe3O4@ MSN-Erlotinib/PEI-FA-had a higher level of cytotoxicity on HeLa cells due to the presence of folic acid in this formulation than Fe3O4@MSN-Erlotinib/PEI [62].

In the current study, we designed and characterized a smart drug delivery system based on GQDs-Fe3O4, which is functionalized by folic acid to enhance the efficiency of curcumin as a non-toxic and hydrophobic anticancer drug. First, GQDs were produced by the disintegration of graphene oxide, and then GQDs-Fe3O4 nanocomposite was prepared using a one-step co-precipitation process. A magnet efficiently separated the nanoparticles. Folic acid was conjugated to the nanocomposite by using EDC/NHS activators. To explore the pH sensitivity of the as-synthesized nanosystem, the release mechanism of curcumin was investigated at pH values 5.5 and 7.4 (Fig. 1 ). Also, the MTT assay test was used to examine the nanocarrier's inhibitory effect on breast cancer (MCF-7) and osteosarcoma (MG-63) cells. Our results revealed that the GQDs-Fe3O4-FA nanosystem could be applied as a promising pH-responsive and cost-effective nanosystem for the targeted delivery of hydrophobic anticancer drugs.

Graphite powder was obtained from Fluka. Iron (III) chloride hexahydrate (FeCl3·6H2O), Iron (II) sulfate heptahydrate (FeSO4·7H2O), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), phosphoric acid (H3PO4), sulfuric acid (H2SO4), hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), potassium permanganate (KMnO4), hydrochloric acid (HCl), ammonia (NH3), folic acid, 1-Ethyl-3-(3-dimethylamino propyl)-carbodiimide(EDC), N-hydroxysulfoxuccinimide (sulfo-NHS), ethanol, and 3-(4,5-Dimethylthiazol-2-yl)-2,5-diphenyltetrazolium bromide (MTT) were purchased from Merck. Fetal bovine serum (FBS), trypsin (0.25% (w/v)) and DMEM medium were purchased from Gibco BRL. The dialysis membrane (3.5 kDa) was purchased from Spectrum Labs Co. The MCF-7 and MG-63 cell lines were purchased from Pasteur Institute, Iran. Human primary fibroblast was obtained from Cell Tech Pharmed Co. (Tehran, Iran). All of the materials were analytical grade and were utilized without being purified. Deionized water (DI) and phosphate buffer saline (with a pH of 7.4) was used to prepare the solutions.

Fourier Transform Infra-Red spectra (Bruker Vector 22 FT-IR spectrometer) were obtained in the range of 400–4000 cm−1 using a KBr pellet method at room temperature. A Vibrant Sample Magnetometer (VSM, Meghnatis Daghigh Kavir Co.) was applied to measure the magnetizations versus the field. Fe3O4 and GQDs-Fe3O4 X-ray diffraction patterns were analyzed using an X-ray diffractometer (XRD, Philips PW 1730/10) with Cu Kα radiation for analyzing the crystalline phase. The scanning is through a range of 10–80°, and the wavelength of the device is 1.5406°A. A model CM300 transmission electron microscope (TEM, Philips) and model VP-50 field emission scanning electron microscopy (FE-SEM, Zeiss SIGMA) were utilized to investigate the samples' morphological and structural properties. Before analysis, the materials were filtered and sonicated over a 0.22 μm membrane. The absorbance of solutions was assessed using a microplate reader spectrophotometer (BioTek Instruments).

GQDs were obtained from graphene oxide (GO), which was synthesized by improved Hummers' method [63]. Briefly, GO (5 mL, 5 mg/mL) was initially sonicated for about 20 min to become homogenous and then was heated up to 55 °C [64]. In the next step, H2O2 (40 mL, 30%) was added to the mixture, followed by 10 mL of ammonia dropwise, under continuous stirring for 24 h. Finally, a cellulose acetate membrane (0.22 m) was used to filter the yellowish-brown mixture.

Magnetic GQDs were fabricated using a one-step co-precipitation method. The filtered GQDs were dispersed in water and sonicated for about 18 min to become homogenous. In a typical experiment, FeCl3·6H2O (0.61 g) was added to the graphene quantum dots (100 mL, 0.5 mg/mL) dispersion at room temperature, under a nitrogen atmosphere, and continuous stirring. Afterward, the temperature was raised to 80 °C, and FeSO4·4H2O (0.338 g) was added to the solution and stirred for an additional 30 min. At the end of the reaction, ammonia was added dropwise to the solution to adjust the pH to 11. The black precipitate was collected with a magnet and washed with DI water several times. The product was kept at 4 °C for characterization analysis [65].

To conjugate the folic acid to the GQDs-Fe3O4 hybrid, EDC/NHS was used as an activating agent [66]. In this method, the carboxylic acid groups of the hybrid mixture are activated by EDC to create amide bonds with the amine groups of the folic acid. The conjugation of folic acid was accomplished by mixing GQDs-Fe3O4 (7 mL, 0.5 mg/mL) with EDC (96 mg) and NHS (59 mg). After stirring for 2 h, folic acid (10.5 mg) was added to the mixture and continued stirring for 20 h at room temperature and dark place to prevent photo-degradation. The excess FA, unreacted EDC, and NHS were removed after repeatedly washing with DI water and applying a magnet. The product was freeze-dried and stored at 4 °C for characterization analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Loading of curcumin on GQDs-Fe3O4 and GQDs-Fe3O4-FA
ce:section.ce:para: Different concentrations of curcumin (0.5, 1, 2, 4 mg) were combined with GQDs-Fe3O4 (0.5 mg. mL−1) and stirred for 24 h at room temperature in the dark place to load Cur on the GQDs-Fe3O4. Afterward, a magnet was used to separate the product, and it was then rinsed three times with a mixture of deionized water and ethanol until the supernatant became color free. The indirect method was used to determine the amount of loaded Cur in the supernatant at the absorbance of 430 nm. The same method was used to load the Cur onto the GQDs-Fe3O4-FA.

The Cur encapsulation efficiency was calculated as follows:

In this equation (Eq. (1)), Wtotal Cur and Wfree Cur refer to the initial weights of Cur and the weight of unbounded Cur after the equilibration condition, respectively.

The release mechanism of Cur from the GQDs-Fe3O4 nanocarrier was carried out by dispersing Cur/GQDs-Fe3O4 (0.5 mg/1 mg) in a dialysis bag (3.5 kDa). Then, it was dialyzed against PBS buffer (100 mL, 150 mM, pH = 5.5, 37 °C) under constant shaking (100 rpm). Due to curcumin's poor solubility in PBS, tween-80 was added to the buffer solution at a concentration of 0.5% w/v to facilitate Cur release [64]. The same method was used to study the release behavior of Cur from the GQDs-Fe3O4-FA.

At scheduled time intervals, an aliquot containing the drug release medium (∼3 mL) was withdrawn for UV analysis, which was replaced with 3 mL of fresh media. Finally, the amount of released Cur in the supernatant of nanoparticles was determined by UV–visible spectrophotometer. The percentage of drug release was calculated as follows [64]:

In the above equation (Eq. (2)), W is the amount of cumulative drug release; Mt and Ct are referred to as the initial amount of Cur and the calculated concentration of Cur at the predetermined time t, respectively. Also, V0 and V1 represent the total volume of the release medium and the sample volume extracted at time t, respectively. The same testing was performed for pH 7.4.

The toxicity of the fabricated nanoparticles on human primary fibroblast as a normal cell line and anticancer effect of different compounds of Cur (free Cur, Cur/GQDs-Fe3O4, Cur/GQDs-Fe3O4-FA) on two cancerous cell lines of MCF-7 and MG-63 were evaluated using MTT technique. Cells were incubated into 96-well plates and cultured in RPMI 1640 supplemented with 10% FBS and 1% penicillin-streptomycin. The concentration of cells was 5⨯103, 3⨯103, and 10⨯103 cells in each well for MCF-7, MG-63, and fibroblast, respectively. After 24 h, the cancer cells were treated with free curcumin, Cur/GQDs-Fe3O4, Cur/GQDs-Fe3O4-FA (final Cur concentrations: 80, 40, 20, 10, and 5 μg/mL), and normal cells were incubated with 50, 25, and 12.5 μg/mL of bare GQDs-Fe3O4 for 48 h. The untreated cells were regarded as control cells. The culture media was removed at the end of the procedure, and the wells were washed twice with PBS. The cells were incubated for 3 h at 37 °C and 5% CO2 in the phenol red-free RPMI 1640 with 0.5 mg/mL MTT. After this period, the medium was aspirated and replaced by DMSO to dissolve the formazan crystals obtained due to MTT metabolization. The plate was gently shaken for 20 min at 37 °C, and the absorbance of this solution at 570 and 630 nm was measured in a microplate reader. The number of viable cells was calculated using the calibration curves between absorbance values and cell numbers. The percentage of viable tests was assessed using the ratio of treated cells to untreated control cells.

The surface functional groups of GQDs, Fe3O4, GQDs-Fe3O4, and GQDs-Fe3O4-FA were studied using FTIR spectroscopy. As shown in Fig. 2 a, in the spectra of Fe3O4, the appearance of the typical characteristic peak of Fe–O at around 581 cm−1 suggests the formation of magnetite [67]. The FTIR spectra of GQDs show peaks at 1386 cm−1 and 1637 cm−1 due to the –OH stretching vibration of the –COOH group and CC, respectively [64]. The peak observed at 1120 cm−1 corresponds to C–N stretching groups. Wide bands between 3000 and 3450 cm−1 are attributed to the stretching vibration of –OH and –NH. Almost all the characteristic peaks of GQDs and the Fe3O4 nanoparticles can be observed in the GQDs-Fe3O4 spectrum, which revealed the successful formation of the GQD-Fe3O4 hybrid. FTIR also confirmed the conjugation of folic acid to GQD-Fe3O4 nanoparticles (Fig. 2a). The FTIR spectra of the GQDs-Fe3O4-FA show strong peaks at 1640 and 1400 cm−1 that are representative peaks of the FA and correspond to the aromatic ring stretch of the pteridine ring and p-amino benzoic acid moiety, respectively.

The magnetic properties of bare Fe3O4 and GQDs-Fe3O4 hybrid were characterized at room temperature by VSM and are presented in Fig. 2b. The remanence (Mr) and coercivity (Hc) for both nanoparticles are close to zero, indicating the characteristic of superparamagnetism [68]. Furthermore, the saturation magnetization (Ms) is determined to be 75.9 emu/g and 60.6 emu/g for Fe3O4 and GQDs-Fe3O4, respectively. The lower magnetism value of GQDs-Fe3O4 compared to bare Fe3O4 can be related to the presence of GQDs with no magnetic properties. Despite the decrease in saturation magnetization of the GQDs-Fe3O4, its magnetic separation can be quickly done by an external magnet.

The crystallography of Fe3O4 and GQDs-Fe3O4 nanoparticles was investigated by XRD, shown in Fig. 2c. Six peaks appeared at 2θ values of 30.12°, 35.39°, 43.04°, 53.58°, 56.93°, and 62.73°. These peaks correspond to pages (220), (311), (400), (422), (511), and (440) planes and affirm the formation of Fe3O4 nanoparticles [68]. The same pattern of GQDs-Fe3O4 indicates that the crystalline structure of magnetite is unaffected by the hybridization process. A further explanation for the lack of GQD signal comes from the small amounts, high dispersion, and low crystallinity of GQDs in GQDs-Fe3O4 nanocomposite [69,70].

FE-SEM (Fig. 3 a) and TEM (Fig. 3b and c) analysis were utilized to characterize the surface morphology and size of the created GQDs-Fe3O4. The TEM image reveals the average particle size of GQDs-Fe3O4 is ∼23 nm. As depicted in the FESEM image, the shape of the formed nanoparticles is spherical, and in some sites, they are aggregated. The mean size of particles is 34.3 nm. The origin of the differences in the particle sizes obtained by SEM and TEM images can be understood from the step of sample supply for these analyses. For SEM analysis, the powder is stuck onto the SEM stub and it is coated by Au or other conductors for nonconductive materials. In the case of TEM analysis, the desired material is usually suspended in a solvent by a high-power ultrasonic instrument to spread the sample well. Then, the suspension was put onto the TEM grid, and let the solution air dry to obtain a thinly sliced sample. Therefore, the images obtained from TEM analysis have higher resolution and the agglomerations could be distinguished with this instrument, while in SEM images, the agglomerated particles are usually observed with larger sizes [71]. As seen in Fig. 3a, the nearly spherical particles have agglomerated and the boundaries are observed. During the sample supply for the TEM analysis, the operator sonicated the sample in a solvent to spread over the grid. That is why the real particle sizes are observed in Fig. 3(b and c).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Curcumin release from Cur/GQDs-Fe3O4 and Cur/GQDs-Fe3O4-FA
ce:section.ce:para: As shown in Fig. 4 , the Cur is released sustainably from synthesized nanocarriers. Long-term drug delivery can be achieved using a sustained-release system [72]. This drug delivery system is useful for drugs that are eliminated immediately after administration from the body or rapidly metabolized. The release mechanism of curcumin from GQDs-Fe3O4 and GQDs-Fe3O4-FA was investigated at 37 °C and under different pH 7.4 and pH 5.5, representing the extracellular normal cell condition and the intracellular condition, respectively. Cur/GQDs-Fe3O4 (Fig. 4a), after a 150 h incubation period, about 50% and 20% of Cur are released at pH 5.5 and 7.4, respectively. In contrast to the Cur released from the Cur/GQDs-Fe3O4 nanocarrier, the curcumin released from the Cur/GQDs-Fe3O4-FA (Fig. 4b) showed a slower release rate. For Cur/GQDs-Fe3O4-FA, the total amounts of curcumin released during the study (150 h) are 33% and 15% at pH 5.5 and 7.4, respectively. While for the nanosystem without folic acid, the cumulative release amounts of Cur are 52% and 18% at pH 5.5 and 7.4, respectively. This observation suggests a possible explanation for the reduction in Cur release in the presence of FA. Adding folic acid to this nanocarrier increased the number of hydroxyl bonds. As a result, stronger connections were formed between Cur and GQDs-Fe3O4-FA, slowing the Cur release rate.

According to the data, less than 10% of curcumin was released in the first 6 h at both pH medium conditions, indicating that neither drug delivery system showed a significant burst release. The release rate of Cur was relatively high in the 30 h for both media. This can be explained that in the early hours, the curcumin molecules, which existed on the outer surface of the nanocarrier and did not interact strongly with the system, were separated and released. Furthermore, the slope of the Cur release profile at pH 5.5 gradually decreased, which finally reached a constant amount after 80 h.

In summary, the cumulative release of Cur from both nano-assemblies was greatly affected by the environmental acidity, which means the release rate of Cur was higher at pH 5.5 than at pH 7.4. It is assumed that curcumin was loaded onto GQDs- Fe3O4 and GQDs-Fe3O4-FA via π-π stacking and hydrogen bonding interactions, which are both pH-dependent. These bonds become weaker in an acidic environment because of the protonation of the –OH functional group of Cur molecules [59]. It weakens the hydrogen bond connection between the nanocarrier and drug while increasing the solubility and hydrophilicity of the curcumin. On the contrary, this hydrogen bonding has significant stability in neutral condition (pH 7.4).

To investigate the in vitro release kinetics of Cur from the GQDs-Fe3O4 and GQDs-Fe3O4-FA at the two pH values of 5.5 and 7.4, the four most applied mathematical models, i.e., the first order (Eq. (3)), pseudo-second-order (Eq. (4)), Higuchi (Eq. (5)), and Korsmeyer–Peppas (Eq. (6)) were used. Table 1 shows data related to drug release kinetics at different pH values. Fig. 5 shows the fitting curves of the data in Table 1.

Where Q0, Qe, and Qt are the initial, equilibrium, and time-dependent release rates of curcumin, respectively; k is the release rate constant [64]. The model with the highest correlation coefficient between the fitted data was chosen as the best-fitting model. Curcumin released from both nano-assemblies fits better with the pseudo-second order model.

One of the most significant challenges with conventional drug delivery systems is the lack of distinction between healthy and cancerous cells. Therefore, in this study, fibroblast cells were utilized to assess the toxicity of the GQDs-Fe3O4 and GQDs-Fe3O4-FA nanosystems by MTT assay as a potent and efficient method. It is the most popular test for cell viability, proliferation, and cytotoxicity [73]. As shown in Fig. 6 a, even at high doses (50 μg/mL), normal fibroblast cell lines treated with GQDs-Fe3O4 and GQDs-Fe3O4-FA showed up to 95% cell viability after 48 h of incubation. As a result, there is no evidence of cytotoxicity for nanocarriers without drugs, emphasizing their biocompatibility. Moreover, MTT assay was performed on human breast cancer cell line (MCF-7) and human osteoblast-like cells (MG-63) for anticancer efficacy of Cur/GQDs-Fe3O4, Cur/GQDs-Fe3O4-FA in comparison with free curcumin drug. According to Fig. 6b and c, for free curcumin, the cell viability of MCF-7 and MG-63 cell lines reduced as the Cur concentration increased, until at 80 μg mL−1, the cell viability was decreased to nearly 2%. Additionally, cell viabilities of Cur/GQDs-Fe3O4 and Cur/GQDs-Fe3O4-FA are higher than bare curcumin at high curcumin concentrations (80, 40 μg mL−1). However, at low concentrations of Cur (5, 10, 20 μg mL−1), the cell viabilities of Cur/GQDs-Fe3O4 and Cur/GQDs-Fe3O4-FA are lower than curcumin alone. At lower concentrations of complexes, more cancer cell death suggests a synergistic effect [73]. Comparing cell viability values for Cur/GQDs-Fe3O4 and Cur/GQDs-Fe3O4-FA systems, it is observed that more cells were killed at a particular dosage of curcumin when the targeting agent was utilized than when folic acid was not. For instance, at a concentration of 40 μg/mL Cur, the cell viability of MCF-7 cells treated with Cur/GQDs-Fe3O4 and Cur/GQDs-Fe3O4-FA is 31% and 25%, respectively. Another noteworthy point obtained by comparing the results of the two cell lines is that the cell viability of MG-63 cells was more than that of MCF-7 at a constant concentration, which can be concluded that the cytotoxicity effect of nanocarriers on MCF-7 cells is more than MG-63 cell lines.

This research aimed to evaluate the potential uses of graphene quantum dots-Fe3O4 nanocarrier targeted with folic acid and loaded with curcumin in targeted drug delivery systems. In summary, the GQDs-Fe3O4 were synthesized; folic acid as a targeting agent was conjugated (GQDs- Fe3O4-FA); Curcumin (Cur), as an anticancer drug, was loaded onto the GQDs-Fe3O4-FA. The as-synthesized nanocarrier was characterized via FTIR, XRD, and VSM analysis. The accumulative release profile of Cur was studied and observed it had a pH-dependent and sustained release mechanism. Acidic conditions resulted in far higher release than neutral conditions. Given the tumor's acidic environment, this is desirable. Finally, the MTT assay on MCF-7 and MG-63 cell lines was used to examine the cell viability of the nanocarriers. The higher cytotoxicity again affirms the potential involvement of folic acid as a targeting agent for Cur/GQDs-Fe3O4-FA nanocarrier for both cell lines compared to the Cur/GQDs-Fe3O4. It was also observed that the cytotoxicity for MCF-7 cells was slightly higher than for MG-63. Moreover, to investigate the toxicity of synthesized nanocarriers, MTT analysis was also carried out, and it was revealed that the cytotoxicity effect of GQDs-Fe3O4 and GQDs-Fe3O4-FA against normal cells is significantly low. The newly synthesized nano-assembly is unquestionably a better candidate for drug delivery applications than the free drug.
